talk_title: A Primer for Geospatial Science Analytics in Cancer Research and Translation

speaker.name: Trang Vo-Pham
speaker.affiliation: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center, University of Washington

clinical_data.population: Washington state patients at high risk for air pollution-related health effects

MOA_summary: Geospatial science links residential locations to environmental exposure datasets (e.g., air pollution, green space, socioeconomic indices) enabling epidemiologic analyses of cancer incidence, mortality, and disparities; translational tools such as the GeoExMap web app and EHR-linked outreach convert these insights into community‑focused interventions.
key_findings: Nearly 3,000 cancer‑related geospatial publications were indexed in 2025, demonstrating rapid growth of the field.
Geospatial analyses identified cancer mortality hotspots linked to lower education and mammography screening rates.
Neighborhood deprivation index was positively associated with breast cancer‑specific mortality among non‑Hispanic White women but not Black women.
Higher county‑level trauma measures correlated with increased all‑cancer mortality and lower cancer‑prevention behaviors.
Neighborhood socioeconomic status explained most variation in lung cancer mortality, whereas genetics dominated breast and prostate cancer mortality.
County‑level wildfire‑related PM2.5 exposure was associated with ~490 additional cancer deaths per year per 0.1 µg/m³ increase.
The GeoExMap open‑source web app provides interactive mapping of >175 health and environmental variables with community‑tailored mitigation tips.

talk_title: Environmental Exposures Impact on Cancer Risk and Survival

speaker.name: Jamie Hart
speaker.affiliation: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, Brigham and Women's Hospital

clinical_data.population: cancer patients exposed to wildfire smoke

clinical_data.value: suggestive elevated risk, driven by premenopausal and Black/Non‑Hispanic White women

MOA_summary: Fine particulate matter (PM2.5) and traffic‑related nitrogen dioxide (NO2) generate oxidative stress, chronic inflammation, DNA adducts and epigenetic alterations that increase cancer initiation and progression; wildfire‑derived PM2.5 appears more toxic, amplifying mortality risk in exposed cancer patients.
key_findings: A 10 µg/m³ increase in PM2.5 is associated with a 13% higher lung cancer incidence across global cohorts.
Wildfire‑specific PM2.5 exposure adds ~490 cancer deaths per year per 0.1 µg/m³ increase, indicating higher toxicity than background PM2.5.
NO2 exposure shows a suggestive elevated risk for premenopausal breast cancer, especially among Black and non‑Hispanic White women.
Recent studies are shifting from total PM2.5 to source‑specific components (e.g., metal content, wildfire smoke) to pinpoint carcinogenic drivers.
Survival analyses using SEER‑Medicare data reveal that patients in high‑wildfire PM2.5 regions experience worse cancer‑specific mortality.
open_questions: How can source‑specific air pollution components be integrated into risk prediction models for diverse cancer types?

talk_title: Impact of Structural and Social Drivers of Health on Cancer Risk and Survival

models.name: RESPOND Study (U19)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 10 cancer registries across 8 states, >82,000 Black men, >314,000 White men

clinical_data.value: up to ~2.0 in highest redlining quintile
clinical_data.n: n=82,000 Black men; n=314,000 White men
clinical_data.population: African American and non‑Hispanic White men with prostate cancer
Black breast cancer patients in North Carolina

MOA_summary: Structural racism manifested as redlining and multi‑domain segregation creates concentrated socioeconomic deprivation, leading to higher exposure to adverse environmental factors and limited healthcare access, which together increase tumor aggressiveness and mortality in affected populations.
key_findings: Black men with prostate cancer residing in the highest redlining neighborhoods have nearly double the hazard of prostate cancer mortality compared with White men in non‑redlined areas.

Across multiple states (e.g., California, Georgia, Louisiana, Detroit, New Jersey, Texas, Florida), a dose‑response relationship between redlining intensity and prostate cancer mortality was observed.

A composite county‑level structural racism index (housing, education, employment, income, health) was associated with higher odds of treatment delay among Black breast cancer patients, but not among White patients.
Multi‑domain structural racism measures reveal heterogeneous impacts across cancer types, underscoring the need for race‑specific composite indices.
Community advisory boards guided the selection of geospatial variables, ensuring relevance to lived experiences and facilitating translation of findings.
open_questions: Do historical redlining patterns produce similar mortality gradients as contemporary redlining?
Can interventions targeting housing discrimination reduce observed survival disparities?
